county_metadata.research_tips: Goshen County Clerk in Torrington has records from 1911. Fort Laramie National Historic Site preserves military records and fur trade documentation — NARA has fort records from 1849-1890. The Oregon Trail, California Trail, and Mormon Trail all passed through Goshen County; emigrant registers at Fort Laramie are at NARA. Sugar beet farming attracted German-Russian immigrants; Holly Sugar Company records may identify workers. Homestead Valley Museum in Torrington has local genealogy files. Check Laramie County for pre-1911 records. Irrigation district records document water rights holders.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911 from parts of Big Horn and Fremont counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hot Springs County Clerk in Thermopolis holds records from 1911. Named for the hot springs purchased from the Shoshone and Arapaho in the 1896 treaty — treaty documents at NARA shed light on early settlement. Hot Springs County Museum and Cultural Center in Thermopolis has pioneer family files. Owl Creek and Wind River irrigation districts brought homesteaders; water rights records at the State Engineer's Office identify settlers. Check Big Horn and Fremont counties for pre-1911 records. Small county population means many families are well-documented in local newspaper archives.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875 from Carbon County. Site of the Johnson County War (1892).
county_metadata.research_tips: Johnson County Clerk in Buffalo has records from 1875. The Johnson County War (1892) between cattle barons and homesteaders produced extensive court records, military intervention records, and newspaper coverage — trial documents at the WY State Archives. Fort McKinney military post records at NARA. Jim Gatchell Memorial Museum in Buffalo has outstanding western history and family files. The Bozeman Trail crossed Johnson County; military campaigns against the Lakota and Cheyenne (1860s-80s) are documented at NARA. TA Ranch and other large cattle operations have surviving business records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1867 before Wyoming Territory was formally organized (1868). Original county of Wyoming Territory. Parent to many other counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Laramie County Clerk in Cheyenne has the oldest county records in Wyoming, dating to 1867. Wyoming State Archives and State Historical Society in Cheyenne are the central state repositories for territorial and state records. Cheyenne was founded as a Union Pacific railroad town in 1867 — UP records and depot files document early residents. Francis E. Warren Air Force Base (originally Fort D.A. Russell, 1867) military records at NARA. Cheyenne Frontier Days records date to 1897 and document rodeo participants. The Cheyenne Genealogical Society and Laramie County Library genealogy room are major resources. Territorial legislature records name early civic leaders.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911 from Uinta County. Sublette and Teton counties later carved from Lincoln.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County Clerk in Kemmerer maintains records from 1911. J.C. Penney opened his first 'Golden Rule' store in Kemmerer in 1902 — early business records document the community. Fossil Butte National Monument area has historical homestead records. The Oregon Trail's Sublette Cutoff crossed Lincoln County; emigrant records at NARA. Star Valley in western Lincoln County was settled by Mormon families from Idaho and Utah — LDS church records on FamilySearch are essential. Check Uinta County for pre-1911 records. Coal mining in Kemmerer and Diamondville employed many immigrants.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1888 from Carbon County. Named for natron (sodium) deposits.
county_metadata.research_tips: Natrona County Clerk in Casper has records from 1888. Casper became Wyoming's oil capital with the Salt Creek oil field (discovered 1889) and Teapot Dome — oil company employment records and lease files at WY State Archives and NARA identify workers and landowners. Fort Caspar Museum has military records and emigrant trail documents. The Platte Bridge Station fight (1865) is documented at NARA. Natrona County Public Library genealogy collection is strong. The Midwest Refinery and Standard Oil operations brought diverse workers. Casper College's Western History Center has oral histories and family papers. Mormon handcart disaster at Martin's Cove (1856) documented in LDS records.

county_metadata.research_tips: Niobrara County Clerk in Lusk holds records from 1911. The Stagecoach Museum in Lusk has Cheyenne-Deadwood stage route artifacts and pioneer family records. Niobrara County is one of Wyoming's least populated — early homestead records are sparse but well-preserved. The Cheyenne-Deadwood Trail passed through; stage station records may document early residents. Hat Creek Stage Station records survive. Check Converse County for pre-1911 records. Ranching dominates county history; brand books and stock growers' association records at the WY State Archives identify early families. Lance Creek oil field records at State Oil and Gas Commission.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1909 from Big Horn County. Named for Yellowstone National Park.
county_metadata.research_tips: Park County Clerk in Cody maintains records from 1909. Buffalo Bill Historical Center (now Buffalo Bill Center of the West) in Cody holds one of the finest western history collections in the nation, including family papers, photographs, and Wild West Show records. William F. 'Buffalo Bill' Cody founded the town in 1896; his colonization company records document early settlers. Yellowstone National Park records at NARA include early concessionaire and ranger records. Shoshone National Forest records at NARA. The Shoshone Irrigation Project (Bureau of Reclamation) brought homesteaders; project records at NARA Denver. Check Big Horn County for pre-1909 records.
